details.text: Derivative: الْخَرَّاصُونَ refers to those who continue to make wild guesses or form conjectures from خَرَصَ.
Derivative: غَمْرَةٍ from غَمَرَ means a deluge, flood, immersion or state of being overwhelmed.
Derivative: سَاهُونَ to be heedless of what they have been commanded with.
Derivative: أَيَّانَ means “when” but is a question form that is used in a state of frustration.
Derivative: يَهْجَعُونَ from هَجَعَ – هُجُوعٌ which means to be still without movement, and it refers to the state of being asleep.

the first mention of سلام is in the accusative “mansoob” case with two fat’hahs (سَلَامًا). the second mention is in the nominative “marfoo’” case with two dammahs (سَلَامٌ). the first is part of a verbal sentence (الجُمْلَة الاِسْمِيَّة) and the second is part of a noun-based or nominal sentence (الجُمْلَةُ الاِسْمِيَّة). as the nominal sentence is more powerful or far reaching than the verbal sentence, it implies that the second salam had more to it than the first e.g., the first was as-salamu alaikum and the second was as-salamu alaikum wa rahmatullah wa barakatuhu.

Derivative: الْعَقِيمَ means to be barren and refers to something that doesn’t produce any fruits. Previously in this Surah, the wife of Ibrahim (as) said that she was عَقِيمٌ (‘Aqeem i.e., unable to bare children) and here the wind (الرِّيحَ) is being described as الْعَقِيمَ (Al-‘Aqeem) referring to wind that brings to Rizq (provision and sustenance) in it i.e., it has nothing beneficial in it.
Derivative: الرَّمِيمِ is something that has deteriorated and decayed so that it becomes like disintegrated ruins that are fragile or turned to dust and powder.
Derivative: عَتَوْا from عَتَا – عُتُوٌ which means to be insolent and rebellious i.e., to turn away in disobedience with arrogance.
Derivative: أَيْدٍ means hands and is an expression to imply power.

وَالسَّمَاءَ بَنَيْنَاهَا

- وَالسَّمَاءَ بَنَيْنَاهَا “And the sky or heaven, we built it”. Here, the sentence begins with the مَفْعول بِهِ (Maf’ool Bihi – The Object of the verb) which is السَّمَاءَ (the sky or heaven), followed by the verb with the فَاعِل (Faa’il – Doer) which is بَنَيْنَا with the هَا attached بَنَيْنَاهَا which goes back to the مَفْعول بِهِ (Maf’ool Bihi – The Object of the verb). The object of the verb is therefore mentioned twice and the purpose of this style is to place greater emphasis and bring greater attention to the accomplishment of the doer i.e., by mentioning the accomplishment twice.
Derivative: فَرَشْنَا from فَرَشَ which means to lay or spread out. فَرْش (Farsh) is a comfortable flooring.
Derivative: الْمَاهِدُونَ those who smooth, spread and level out. Who make it comfortable and prepared like the مَهْد (Mahd – Cradle) of the mother.

Derivative: تَوَاصَوْا from تَوَاصَى – تَواصٍ and it comes from وَصِيَةٌ (Wasiyah) which means to leave a will or bequeath something. تَوَاصَى means to leave advice, directions or responsibilities behind. Here, in this verse, it is being used to say that the same response of dealing with Messengers, by saying that they are sorcerers or mad men, is as if it has been passed down from one generation to another, due to its repetition i.e., تَوَاصَوْا means to pass down the same instruction from generation to generation.
Derivative: مَلُومٍ from لَامَ – يَلُومُ which means to blame someone or something. مَلُومٍ means the one who is blamed or found to be blameworthy.

Derivative: ذَنُوبًا refers to a bucket that is used to draw water out of a well and here it can refer to the efforts that they make every day which they believe are in their favour but in the end are actually the opposite (Note: Strange interpretation from Ustadh Nu’man here. The following is how it was interpreted by others). It also refers to a share or portion of something. Here it refers to a portion or share of punishment in the hereafter.
Derivative: الْحُبُكِ means pathways and refers to the paths in which the stars move and proceed in.
